title: Eastern water dragon

infobox.value: (***Intellagama lesueurii**)*,[[2]](#cite_note-Amey-2) which includes the **eastern water dragon** (*Intellagama lesueurii lesueurii)* and the **Gippsland water dragon** (*Intellagama lesueurii howittii)* [subspecies](/wiki/Subspecies "Subspecies"), is a [semi aquatic](/wiki/Semi_aquatic "Semi aquatic") [agamid](/wiki/Agamid "Agamid") [species](/wiki/Species "Species") native to eastern [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") from [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") northwards to [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"). There may be a small introduced population on the south-east coast of [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia").

Peters"), 1866   + *Amphibolurus branchialis*  [De Vis](/wiki/De_Vis "De Vis"), 1884   + *Physignathus lesueurii*  — [Boulenger](/wiki/George_Albert_Boulenger "George Albert Boulenger"), 1885   + *Intellagama lesueurii*  — Wells & Wellington, 1985 | |

The **Australian water dragon** (***Intellagama lesueurii**)*,[[2]](#cite_note-Amey-2) which includes the **eastern water dragon** (*Intellagama lesueurii lesueurii)* and the **Gippsland water dragon** (*Intellagama lesueurii howittii)* [subspecies](/wiki/Subspecies "Subspecies"), is a [semi aquatic](/wiki/Semi_aquatic "Semi aquatic") [agamid](/wiki/Agamid "Agamid") [species](/wiki/Species "Species") native to eastern [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") from [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)") northwards to [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"). There may be a small introduced population on the south-east coast of [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia").
The Gippsland water dragon is generally the more southern of the two subspecies and the more cold adapted and heat sensitive.
Visually distinguishing the Gippsland water dragon from the Eastern water dragon is relatively easy, as long as their skin is reasonably clean and not stained from the water, as identification of the two subspecies depends largely on observable differences in colours and patterns.

The Gippsland water dragon may be distinguished by its green-blue colour, especially during the breeding season, when this overall colouration is quite distinct. Another key difference is the absence of a prominent dark stripe behind the eye in the Gippsland water dragon, which is characteristic of the Eastern water dragon. The gular region of the two subspecies is also quite different, with *Intellagama l. howittii* having orange-yellow streaked with darker striping, which is sometimes quite striking, particularly in mature males, whereas *Intellagama l. lesueurii* typically has a pale, unmarked throat that is sometimes immaculate white in mature males. The belly and chest of *Intellagama l. howittii* are also quite different to *Intellagama l. lesueurii*, usually being blackish green, especially in mature males, and the limbs are often quite dark, sometimes even black. Whereas in *Intellagama l. lesueurii* the chest and belly are usually bright to deep red, particularly in mature males. *Intellagama l. lesueurii* also have relatively strong dark transverse bars across the back, whereas these dark bars are often much reduced in the Gippsland water dragon.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The [specific name](/wiki/Specific_name_(zoology) "Specific name (zoology)"), *lesueurii*, is in honor of French [naturalist](/wiki/Naturalist "Naturalist") [Charles Alexandre Lesueur](/wiki/Charles_Alexandre_Lesueur "Charles Alexandre Lesueur").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Taxonomy and systematics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Taxonomy and systematics")]

The species was first described by [John Edward Gray](/wiki/John_Edward_Gray "John Edward Gray") in 1831 as *Lophura lesueurii*, from a specimen collected by [Lesueur](/wiki/Charles_Alexandre_Lesueur "Charles Alexandre Lesueur") & [Péron](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_P%C3%A9ron "François Péron") at "Parramatta" or "Port Jackson".[[5]](#cite_note-Gray1831-5)[[6]](#cite_note-MNHN-6) Gray listed three species of *Lophura*: Cuvier's (*Lophura Cuvierii* Gray), Lesueur's (*Lophura Lesueurii* Gray), and beautiful (*Lophura Concinna* Gray, *Physignathus Concinnus*[[a]](#cite_note-7) Cuvier).[[5]](#cite_note-Gray1831-5)

In 1845,[[7]](#cite_note-8) Gray separated *Physignathus* (*P. concinnus* & *P. Lesueurii*) from *Lophura* (*L. amboinensis*[[b]](#cite_note-9) & *L. Shawii*[[c]](#cite_note-10)). The Australian water dragon remained in *Physignathus* along with the [Asian water dragon](/wiki/Asian_water_dragon "Asian water dragon") *P. cocincinus* until [Wells and Wellington](/wiki/Wells_and_Wellington_affair "Wells and Wellington affair") published the genus *Intellagama* in 1985.

The subspecies *howitii* was described by [Frederick McCoy](/wiki/Frederick_McCoy "Frederick McCoy") in 1884 as "the Gippsland water lizard". According to his description, it differs from the Queensland subspecies in the proportions of the head and the supra-ocular scales. Its sub-specific epithet commemorates geologist and magistrate [Alfred William Howitt](/wiki/Alfred_William_Howitt "Alfred William Howitt"), who collected three specimens from the upper reaches of the [Buchan River](/wiki/Buchan_River "Buchan River") and sent them to McCoy.[[8]](#cite_note-McCoy1884-11) Two of the specimens cannot be located,[[9]](#cite_note-Coventry1970-12) the third is D1822[[10]](#cite_note-MVC2023-13) in the collection of the National Museum of Victoria, which was designated the [lectotype](/wiki/Lectotype "Lectotype") by Coventry in 1970.[[9]](#cite_note-Coventry1970-12)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Description")]

Australian water dragons have long powerful limbs and claws for climbing, a long muscular laterally-compressed tail for swimming, and prominent [nuchal](/wiki/Nuchal "Nuchal") and vertebral crests.[[11]](#cite_note-JCUP-14) (A nuchal crest is a central row of spikes at the base of the head. These spikes continue down the spine, getting smaller as they reach the base of the tail.)[[12]](#cite_note-anbgD-15)

Including their tails, which comprise about two-thirds of their total length, adult females grow to about 60 cm (2 feet) long, and adult males can grow slightly longer than one metre (39 inches) and weigh about 1 kg. Males show bolder colouration and have larger heads than females.[[13]](#cite_note-Willa-16)[[14]](#cite_note-17) Colour is less distinct in juveniles.[[15]](#cite_note-Whispa-18)

### Species variation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Species variation")]

The Australian water [dragon](/wiki/Dragon "Dragon") is the only [species](/wiki/Species "Species") of the genus *Intellagama*.[[2]](#cite_note-Amey-2)

There are two [subspecies](/wiki/Subspecies "Subspecies"); *Intellagama lesueurii lesueurii* (eastern water dragon) and *Intellagama lesueurii howittii* ([Gippsland](/wiki/Gippsland "Gippsland") water dragon). The nominate subspecies *Intellagama lesueurii lesueurii* has a dark band behind its eye and an essentially un-patterned throat that tends towards white and is often immaculate in adult males. Whereas the subspecies *Intellagama lesueurii howittii* lacks the dark stripe behind the eye and has a patterned throat, which is lightly patterned in females but boldly patterned and coloured in adult males. This patterning usually consists of a dark stripe on either side of its throat, and blotched with orange, yellow or blue towards the centre of the throat. Both subspecies are light greenish grey in overall colour (greener in I. l. howittii), with darker bands running across their back, tail and legs. The water dragon can slowly change skin colour to aid its camouflage. The skin will shed during periods of growth and seasonally to reveal brighter colours in Spring that may aid breeding success.

## Behaviour

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Behaviour")]

Head-bobbing behaviour

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Water_Dragon2.jpg/250px-Water_Dragon2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Water_Dragon2.jpg)

Eastern water dragon

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Physignathus_lesueurii_howittii.jpg/250px-Physignathus_lesueurii_howittii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Physignathus_lesueurii_howittii.jpg)

*Intellagama lesueurii howitti*, Gippsland water dragon basking in [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra")

Australian water dragons are extremely shy in the wild, but readily adapt to continual human presence in suburban parks and gardens. They are fast runners and strong climbers. When faced with a potential predator, they seek cover in thick vegetation, or drop from an overhanging branch into water. They are able to swim totally submerged, and rest on the bottom of shallow creeks or lakes for up to 90 minutes,[[12]](#cite_note-anbgD-15) to avoid detection.

Both males and females display typical [agamid](/wiki/Agamid "Agamid") behaviour such as [basking](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bask "wikt:bask"), arm-waving and head-bobbing. Fast arm-waving signals dominance, while slow arm-waving signals submission. Males are territorial,[[11]](#cite_note-JCUP-14) and in areas of higher population density, males exhibit displays of aggression toward other males including posturing, chasing and fighting.

### Breeding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Breeding")]

Australian water dragons living in cooler Australian [climates](/wiki/Climates "Climates") [hibernate](/wiki/Hibernate "Hibernate") over winter. During spring, usually in early October, the [female](/wiki/Female "Female") excavates a burrow about 10–15 cm (3.9–5.9 in) deep and lays between 6 and 18 eggs.[[11]](#cite_note-JCUP-14) The nest is usually in sandy or soft [soil](/wiki/Soil "Soil"), in an area open to sun. When the mother has laid the eggs, she backfills the chamber with soil and scatters loose debris over it. Australian water dragons exhibit temperature dependent [sex](/wiki/Sex "Sex") determination; the sex of the [hatchlings](/wiki/Hatchlings "Hatchlings") is determined by the temperature of the nest site.[[12]](#cite_note-anbgD-15)

When the young are hatched, they stay near the entrance of the burrow for some time before leaving home. When they finally leave the nest, they tend to group together away from the adult population.[[13]](#cite_note-Willa-16)

## Habitat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Habitat")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Basking_Australian_water_dragon_in_City_Botanic_Gardens_Brisbane_Australia.jpg/250px-Basking_Australian_water_dragon_in_City_Botanic_Gardens_Brisbane_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basking_Australian_water_dragon_in_City_Botanic_Gardens_Brisbane_Australia.jpg)

[Basking](/wiki/Sunning_(behaviour) "Sunning (behaviour)") water dragon in Brisbane's [City Botanic Gardens](/wiki/City_Botanic_Gardens "City Botanic Gardens").

As its [common name](/wiki/Common_name "Common name") suggests, the Australian water dragon is associated with water and is semi-aquatic. It can be found near creeks, rivers, lakes, and other water bodies that have basking sites such as overhanging branches or rocks in open or filtered sun. The species is very common in the rainforest section of [Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mount Coot-tha](/wiki/Brisbane_Botanic_Gardens,_Mount_Coot-tha "Brisbane Botanic Gardens, Mount Coot-tha") in Queensland, and a monument has been built to them there. There is also a significant population in Brisbane's [Roma Street Parklands](/wiki/Roma_Street_Parklands "Roma Street Parklands").

There are anecdotal reports of a small colony living on the Sixth Creek in the [Forest Range](/wiki/Forest_Range,_South_Australia "Forest Range, South Australia") area of South Australia, hundreds of kilometers outside their natural range, which were probably introduced there during the 1980s by a local reptile enthusiast.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Predators, threats and diet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Predators, threats and diet")]

Australian water dragons are prey to carnivorous birds, snakes (such as [brown tree snakes](/wiki/Brown_tree_snake "Brown tree snake"), [common death adders](/wiki/Common_death_adder "Common death adder"), [lowland copperheads](/wiki/Lowland_copperhead "Lowland copperhead"), [red-bellied black snakes](/wiki/Red-bellied_black_snake "Red-bellied black snake"), and [eastern brown snakes](/wiki/Eastern_brown_snake "Eastern brown snake")), cats, dogs, and foxes. Nestlings and smaller juvenile water dragons are vulnerable to predation by [kookaburras](/wiki/Kookaburra "Kookaburra"), [currawongs](/wiki/Currawong "Currawong"), [butcherbirds](/wiki/Butcherbird "Butcherbird") and other carnivorous birds.[[16]](#cite_note-Pred-19) They are also prone to becoming road kill due to the attraction of warm bitumen and concrete for basking.[[16]](#cite_note-Pred-19)
The Australian water dragon's diet depends on its size. Juveniles and yearlings tend to feed on spiders and small insects such as ants, crickets, and caterpillars. When they get bigger, so does their prey. An adult diet includes small rodents, such as baby mice, other reptiles, frogs, fish, crabs, yabbies, molluscs, worms and eggs, although insects are still the most commonly consumed.[[17]](#cite_note-AusMus-20)[[18]](#cite_note-BB-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22)[[20]](#cite_note-AF-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24)[[22]](#cite_note-25) Types of vegetation reportedly consumed include figs, lilly-pilly fruits, berries, and other fruits and flowers.[[17]](#cite_note-AusMus-20)[[18]](#cite_note-BB-21)[[20]](#cite_note-AF-23)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_water_dragon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** Cuvier's original spelling was *[Physignathus cocincinus](/wiki/Physignathus_cocincinus "Physignathus cocincinus")*, after the type locality "cocincine" ([Cochin-China](/wiki/Cochin-China "Cochin-China")).
2. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** The name *[Lophura](/wiki/Lophura "Lophura")* was already used for a genus of pheasants, the current name is *[Hydrosaurus amboinensis](/wiki/Hydrosaurus_amboinensis "Hydrosaurus amboinensis")*.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** *Shawii* now considered to be conspecific with *amboinensis*.
